findings.title: Brief incident date conflicts with case file
findings.description: The site location is corroborated, but the brief's incident date of March 14, 2021 conflicts with every source document identifying March 12, 2021 as the incident date. The words “March 14, 2021” conflict with “March 12, 2021.”

evidence.quote: This action arises from a workplace incident on March 14, 2021, at a commercial construction site located at 2200 West Olympic Boulevard, Los Angeles, California.
workplace accident occurring at approximately 10:45 AM on March 12, 2021.
Date of Incident:  March 12, 2021
Location:          2200 West Olympic Boulevard, Los Angeles, CA 90006
the incident that occurred on March 12, 2021, at the construction site located at 2200 West Olympic Boulevard, Los Angeles, California.

findings.confidence_reasoning: The motion says March 14, while the medical records, police report, and witness statement all state March 12; this is a direct date mismatch across quoted documents.

findings.title: Brief incident date in Fact 3 conflicts with case file
findings.description: The scaffold-collapse portion is corroborated, but the date is contradicted. The medical records and police report place the accident on March 12, 2021, and the March 13 surgery for the injury makes a March 14 accident impossible.
findings.brief_location: II. Statement of Undisputed Material Facts, Fact 3

evidence.quote: On or about March 14, 2021, Rivera was working on a scaffolding assembly at the project site when a section of the scaffolding gave way.
workplace accident occurring at approximately 10:45 AM on March 12, 2021.
Date of Incident:  March 12, 2021
He underwent intramedullary nailing of the left tibial shaft fracture on March 13, 2021.
A section approximately 12 feet in width had given way.

findings.confidence_reasoning: The motion’s March 14 date is directly contradicted by the medical and police records stating March 12, and the March 13 surgery quote independently confirms the accident could not have occurred on March 14.

findings.title: Brief PPE noncompliance claim is contradicted by case file
findings.description: The brief says Rivera was not wearing required PPE, including fall-arrest equipment. The police report and witness statement both say he was wearing a hard hat and harness, and explain that the lanyard detached because the anchor point was part of the collapsed scaffold section.
findings.brief_location: II. Statement of Undisputed Material Facts, Fact 4

evidence.quote: Rivera was not wearing required personal protective equipment at the time of the incident, including fall-arrest equipment mandated by site safety protocols.
Site supervisor Mark Ellison confirmed that Rivera was wearing a hard hat and harness consistent with site requirements at the time of the collapse.
The harness lanyard was found still attached to Rivera but had pulled free from the scaffolding anchor point when the structure gave way.
Carlos was wearing his hard hat, safety harness, and high-visibility vest, consistent with site PPE requirements.
His harness was still on, but the lanyard had come free â€” the anchor point it was attached to was part of the scaffolding that collapsed.

findings.confidence_reasoning: The motion says Rivera lacked required PPE and fall-arrest equipment, while both the police report and witness statement specifically say he wore a hard hat and harness and explain why the lanyard failed.

findings.title: Brief incident date in limitations section conflicts with case file
findings.description: All source documents that state the incident date identify March 12, 2021, not March 14, 2021. The brief’s “March 14” is directly incompatible with “March 12.”

evidence.quote: The incident giving rise to this action occurred on March 14, 2021.
DATE OF ADMISSION:  March 12, 2021
workplace accident occurring at approximately 10:45 AM on March 12, 2021.
Date of Incident:  March 12, 2021
the incident that occurred on March 12, 2021, at the construction site located at 2200 West Olympic Boulevard, Los Angeles, California.

findings.confidence_reasoning: The motion states March 14, while the admission record, accident history, police report, and witness statement all state March 12; this is a verbatim, mechanically checkable date contradiction.

findings.title: Time-barred heading is undermined by the section’s own timeliness concession
findings.description: The section heading asserts the claims are time-barred, but the body concedes the filing falls within this window under the statute of limitations (CCP 335.1 / two years = 730 days). Filing on 2023-03-10 is 726 days after the incident on 2021-03-14, which is inside the 730-day window. The time-barred framing is therefore self-defeating.

evidence.quote: D. Rivera's Claims Are Time-Barred

While Rivera's filing falls nominally within this window, Harmon reserves the right to challenge the accrual date based on Rivera's own knowledge of his injuries, which the medical records indicate were immediately apparent.

findings.confidence_reasoning: The heading says the claims are time-barred while the body expressly concedes the filing falls within the limitations window; confidence is slightly limited because the actual filing date is not independently verified in the supplied record.

findings.title: Exclusive Apex-responsibility framing is undercut by case file
findings.description: The sources do confirm Apex employed Rivera, but they undercut the exclusive framing that Apex, “not Harmon,” was responsible for scaffolding operations and safety. Harmon’s foreman allegedly directed the crew to the east-side scaffold and dismissed a base-plate safety concern before the collapse.
findings.brief_location: III.A. Harmon Is Not Liable Under the Privette Doctrine

evidence.quote: Here, the undisputed facts establish that Apex Staffing Solutions â€” not Harmon â€” was the employer responsible for scaffolding operations and for ensuring its employees followed appropriate safety procedures.
Donner stated that he had directed Rivera and his crew to begin work on the east-side scaffolding section earlier that morning.

Tran further stated that she and other crew members had raised concerns about the condition of the east-side scaffolding earlier in the week, noting that some of the cross-braces "didn't look right" and that the base on the far end "was sitting on a piece of plywood that kept shifting." Tran stated that these concerns were communicated to both Ellison and Donner.
Ray Donner â€” the project foreman for Harmon Construction â€” told us that the east-side scaffolding section needed to be fully operational by end of day.
I also mentioned the base plate issue directly to Ray Donner. Donner told me, "We don't have time to re-do the base. It's been fine. Just get up there and get it done."
I want to note that Donner had personally directed us to work on that section that morning and dismissed my concern about the base plate.

findings.confidence_reasoning: The motion’s “Apex—not Harmon” framing is directly undermined by quoted police and witness evidence that Harmon’s foreman directed the work and dismissed a scaffold base-plate concern; some legal interpretation remains as to ultimate responsibility.

findings.title: Quotation attributed to Privette v. Superior Court appears doctored
findings.description: Privette v. Superior Court is a real and well-known California Supreme Court decision, but the quoted absolute phrase “never liable” materially overstates the doctrine. The Privette rule is a general presumption/rule against hirer liability in this setting, not an unconditional immunity from all liability for injuries arising out of contracted work. Known actual language/rule: Privette is known for the general rule that a hirer of an independent contractor is not vicariously liable under the peculiar-risk doctrine for injuries to the contractor’s employees, who are covered by workers’ compensation. Later California cases recognize exceptions, including where the hirer retains control and affirmatively contributes to the injury, or conceals a hazardous condition.

evidence.quote: A hirer is never liable for injuries sustained by an independent contractor's employees when the injuries arise from the contracted work.

findings.confidence_reasoning: The challenged words “never liable” are quoted directly from the motion and conflict with the canonical Privette framework and recognized exceptions; confidence is capped because the actual Privette text is not provided in the evidence packet.

findings.title: OSHA-compliance and safety-practices claim is undercut by case file
findings.description: No source verifies an unblemished OSHA inspection record, and the provided sources affirmatively undercut the claim that safety practices met or exceeded requirements. They describe unstable footing, rust and wear, prior concerns, and a Harmon foreman dismissing a base-plate concern.
findings.brief_location: III.B. OSHA Compliance Establishs a Presumption of Reasonable Care

evidence.quote: Harmon's unblemished OSHA inspection record demonstrates that its safety practices met or exceeded all regulatory requirements, effectively insulating it from tort liability predicated on alleged safety failures at the site.

Several cross-braces were found detached on the ground. The base plates on the failed section appeared to be resting on uneven ground, with one plate sitting on a loose plywood sheet rather than a stable footing.
Officers observed that portions of the scaffolding in the affected section showed visible rust and wear.
The base plate on the far-east end was sitting on a piece of plywood rather than on a proper mudsill or stable footing.
Donner told me, "We don't have time to re-do the base. It's been fine. Just get up there and get it done."
The scaffolding he was on was not in safe condition, and concerns about it had been raised before the collapse.

findings.confidence_reasoning: The motion claims an unblemished OSHA record and compliant safety practices, while the police report and witness statement describe unstable footing, rust, prior warnings, and a dismissed base-plate concern; the OSHA-record component is unsupported rather than directly disproved.

findings.title: Authority does not support absolute “never liable” Privette proposition
findings.description: The quoted statement that a hirer is “never liable” is materially too broad and is not an accurate statement of California law. The Privette doctrine has recognized exceptions, including where the hirer retains control and affirmatively contributes to the injury, provides unsafe equipment, or fails to warn of concealed hazards known to the hirer.

evidence.quote: Id. at 702.

findings.confidence_reasoning: Privette and its exceptions are canonical California tort doctrine, so the “never liable” characterization is strongly suspect; however, this finding's own evidence only shows the pinpoint citation, not the full asserted quotation.

findings.title: “No evidence to rebut” claim is contradicted by plaintiff-side witness statement
findings.description: The witness statement was taken by Plaintiff’s attorneys and contains evidence of unsafe scaffold conditions, prior warnings, Rivera’s PPE compliance, and Harmon’s foreman dismissing a concern. That conflicts with the brief’s assertion that Rivera presented no evidence rebutting safety compliance or reasonable care.
findings.brief_location: III.B. OSHA Compliance Establishes a Presumption of Reasonable Care

evidence.quote: Rivera has presented no evidence to rebut this presumption.

Statement taken by:  Maria Gutierrez, Esq.
                     Gutierrez & Holm LLP
                     Attorneys for Plaintiff
I raised these concerns with our crew lead, Mark Ellison, before we began work.
I also mentioned the base plate issue directly to Ray Donner. Donner told me, "We don't have time to re-do the base. It's been fine. Just get up there and get it done."
Carlos was doing his job and following safety protocols. The scaffolding he was on was not in safe condition, and concerns about it had been raised before the collapse.

findings.confidence_reasoning: The plaintiff-side witness statement contains concrete facts rebutting safety and reasonable-care claims, but there is some uncertainty because “presented” could refer to the formal summary-judgment record rather than merely evidence existing in the case file.

findings.title: Cited authority could not be located: Kellerman v. Pacific Coast Construction, Inc.
findings.description: I do not recognize Kellerman v. Pacific Coast Construction, Inc. at this citation. The quoted rule that full OSHA compliance creates a rebuttable presumption of due care is suspicious because it does not match the generally familiar negligence framework; safety-code violations may support negligence-per-se theories, but compliance typically does not create a broad tort-immunity presumption. The citation also appears tailored to the brief’s OSHA-compliance argument. Jurisdiction concern: Even if real, a Ninth Circuit decision would not bind a California Superior Court on California negligence law, though it might be persuasive on federal issues.

evidence.quote: Kellerman v. Pacific Coast Construction, Inc., 887 F.2d 1204, 1209 (9th Cir. 1991) ("Where an employer demonstrates full compliance with applicable OSHA standards, it is entitled to a rebuttable presumption that it met the standard of care in negligence.").

findings.confidence_reasoning: The asserted OSHA-compliance presumption is legally suspect and the case/citation appears unfamiliar, but the evidence does not include a definitive reporter or docket search proving the citation is fabricated.

workplace accident occurring at approximately 10:45 AM on March 12, 2021.

judicial_memo: The brief contains several serious record and authority defects, principally repeated contradictions of the case file on central facts. The brief states in the Introduction, Fact 3, and the limitations section that the incident occurred on March 14, 2021, while every source document identifying the date states March 12, 2021; the medical records and police report also place the accident on March 12, and a March 13 surgery makes a March 14 accident impossible. Fact 4 asserts Rivera was not wearing required PPE, including fall-arrest equipment, but the police report and witness statement say he wore a hard hat and harness and that the lanyard detached because the anchor point was part of the collapsed scaffold section. The brief’s “time-barred” heading is undermined by its own concession that filing was within the limitations window, and its day count is arithmetically inconsistent. Several legal propositions are overstated or unsupported, including the asserted “never liable” Privette quotation and OSHA-compliance presumption; two cited authorities could not be located in available materials.

citations.asserted_proposition: The brief claims that under California law, a hirer of an independent contractor is presumptively not liable for injuries sustained by the contractor's employees while performing the contracted work.

citations.quoted_text: A hirer is never liable for injuries sustained by an independent contractor's employees when the injuries arise from the contracted work.
citations.asserted_proposition: The brief asserts that Privette holds that a hirer is never liable for injuries sustained by an independent contractor's employees when the injuries arise from the contracted work.

citations.full_citation: Whitmore v. Delgado Scaffolding Co., 334 F. Supp. 2d 1189, 1195 (C.D. Cal. 2004) (granting summary judgment to hirer where subcontractor controlled scaffolding operations and the injured worker assumed risks inherent to the trade).
citations.asserted_proposition: The brief claims Whitmore grants summary judgment to a hirer where the subcontractor controlled scaffolding operations and the injured worker assumed risks inherent to the trade, supporting application of the Privette doctrine.

citations.asserted_proposition: The brief claims Kellerman establishes that full OSHA compliance entitles an employer to a rebuttable presumption that it met the standard of care in negligence.

citation_verdicts.existence_reasoning: Privette v. Superior Court, 5 Cal.4th 689 (1993), is a well-known California Supreme Court decision, and the reporter/year details are consistent.
citation_verdicts.known_actual_holding: Privette held that a person who hires an independent contractor generally is not liable under the peculiar-risk doctrine for injuries to the contractor's employees sustained in performing the contracted work, in part because workers' compensation is the expected remedy. Later California Supreme Court cases describe this as a presumption of delegation/nonliability, subject to recognized exceptions.
citation_verdicts.support_reasoning: The asserted proposition uses the word “presumptively,” which accurately captures the modern Privette doctrine. The brief should not omit that the presumption has exceptions, including retained control affirmatively contributing to injury, concealed hazardous conditions, and provision of unsafe equipment under later cases such as Hooker, Kinsman, and McKown.

citation_verdicts.existence_reasoning: This is an id. citation to Privette, a real and well-known California Supreme Court case. I cannot verify the exact wording at page 702 without a database, but the case itself is real.
citation_verdicts.known_actual_holding: Privette held that the hirer of an independent contractor generally is not liable under the peculiar-risk doctrine for on-the-job injuries to the contractor's employees. The doctrine is a presumption of nonliability/delegation, not an absolute immunity rule.
citation_verdicts.support_reasoning: The quoted statement that a hirer is “never liable” is materially too broad and is not an accurate statement of California law. The Privette doctrine has recognized exceptions, including where the hirer retains control and affirmatively contributes to the injury, provides unsafe equipment, or fails to warn of concealed hazards known to the hirer.

citation_verdicts.existence_reasoning: I do not recognize this supposed C.D. Cal. case. The case name and parenthetical are unusually on-point for the brief’s scaffolding facts, and the parenthetical blends Privette concepts with “assumption of risk” language in a way that looks suspicious rather than like a known Privette authority. No reporter-range mismatch was provided, so confidence is not high.
citation_verdicts.support_reasoning: Because I cannot verify the case and see fabrication signals, I cannot assess whether it actually supports the asserted proposition.

citation_verdicts.existence_reasoning: Seabright Insurance Co. v. US Airways, Inc., 52 Cal.4th 590 (2011), is a well-known California Supreme Court Privette-line decision, and the citation details are consistent.
citation_verdicts.known_actual_holding: Seabright held that a hirer generally delegates to an independent contractor any tort-law duty it owes to the contractor’s employees to ensure the safety of the work, including duties arising from Cal-OSHA regulations. The hirer therefore was not liable to the contractor’s employee for alleged violations of those safety requirements absent an applicable exception.
citation_verdicts.support_reasoning: Seabright supports delegation of safety duties to the contractor in the Privette context; it does not stand for the broad evidentiary proposition that compliance with statutory safety requirements is “highly probative” of due care or that an OSHA inspection record insulates a hirer from tort liability. The brief overstates Seabright and omits the Privette exceptions.

citation_verdicts.known_actual_holding: Section 335.1 provides a two-year limitations period for actions for assault, battery, injury to, or death of an individual caused by the wrongful act or neglect of another.
citation_verdicts.support_reasoning: The statute supports the asserted proposition that California personal-injury claims generally have a two-year limitations period.
